t: Depending on residual state after substance withdrawal.
First-line psychosocial intervention — MI, CBT, contingency management. Evidence: Davis M.L. et al. Drug Alcohol Depend 2015 meta-analysis.
No specific FDA-approved pharmacotherapy exists. Off-label: N-acetylcysteine (in adolescents Gray K.M. JAMA 2017 small effect RCT), gabapentin.
Comorbid psychosis — antipsychotic; cannabis abstinence critical.
Synthetic cannabinoid intoxication — supportive, atypical antipsychotic if necessary.
NIDA — CBT and contingency management first-line.
Early onset of use — poor prognosis.
CUD develops in 7–9% of lifetime users; 17% in adolescents.
Myth 2: "Cannabis withdrawal syndrome does not exist"
DSM-5 (2013) — withdrawal syndrome as an official category; irritability, sleep, appetite disturbance.
Marconi 2016 — THC increases psychosis risk by 2–3 times; preliminary evidence for CBD, not a clinical recommendation.
"Gateway" concept is controversial, but sequential use pattern is statistically evident; causality has different explanations.
Myth 5: "High-THC cannabis is safer than regular cannabis"
THC dose correlates with risk of psychosis, addiction, cognitive impairment; 'potency creep' over recent decades.
synthetic cannabinoids are very potent, unpredictable; acute psychosis and death cases have been recorded.
WHO. ICD-11. 6C42 Disorders due to use of cannabis. 2024.
Davis M.L. et al. Behavioral therapies for treatment-seeking cannabis users: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. J Addict Dis 2015;34(4):298–312.
Gray K.M. et al. A randomized placebo-controlled trial of N-acetylcysteine for cannabis use disorder in adults. JAMA 2017.
Marconi A. et al. Schizophr Bull 2016;42(5):1262–1269.
